content.assessment: By leaving the selection age and track structure unchanged, this proposal avoids the coordination and workforce-redeployment burdens facing structural alternatives, and it can plausibly be delivered through the existing centralized funding and curriculum-control apparatus (F-live-implementation_planning-06) without requiring the kind of large-scale actor realignment that tracking-age reform would demand. This makes it comparatively more deliverable in the short term. However, it does nothing to address the extreme scale of the equity gap it targets — a 170-point reading gap between top and bottom deciles and only 12.1% of disadvantaged students reaching basic proficiency (F-live-implementation_planning-03) — and compensatory funding alone has no demonstrated mechanism to close a gap of that magnitude while selection and peer-sorting dynamics continue unabated. The teacher-workforce crisis (F-live-implementation_planning-07) also directly threatens the feeder-school teacher-quality mechanism this proposal relies on, since incentivizing placement in disadvantaged schools requires a supply of qualified teachers that is documented as critically short and aging.
opportunities: Centralized financial and curriculum control (F-live-implementation_planning-06) could enable rapid, uniform disbursement of targeted per-pupil funding without needing sub-national administrative coordination
strengths: Requires no structural realignment of selection age or institutional/programme structure, avoiding the coordination problem documented at existing entry points (F-live-implementation_planning-01)
Can be delivered through existing centralized funding and curriculum-control channels without requiring new governance architecture (F-live-implementation_planning-06)
threats: If the qualified-teacher shortage cannot be resolved, targeted incentive funding for disadvantaged-school placement may fail to attract sufficient staff, reproducing the teacher-quality gap the mechanism is meant to close (F-live-implementation_planning-07)

The scale of documented socio-economic inequality (F-live-implementation_planning-03) raises the risk that even fully-implemented compensatory funding proves insufficient, creating a credibility gap between announced intent and measurable outcome
weaknesses: Targets an equity gap of an extremely large documented magnitude (170-point reading gap, only 12.1% of disadvantaged students reaching basic proficiency) with mechanisms whose effect size relative to that gap is unproven (F-live-implementation_planning-03)

Depends on placing qualified teachers in disadvantaged/feeder schools precisely when teacher-training enrollment has fallen ~25% and the workforce is aging sharply, constraining the supply this mechanism requires (F-live-implementation_planning-07)
